summary: Augustine of Hippo (354-430 CE) was a North African bishop and theologian whose writings on grace, sin, the will, and the church shaped Western Christian theology. He is the author of the Confessions, De Trinitate, and De Civitate Dei (City of God).
description: Augustine was born in Thagaste in Roman North Africa and taught rhetoric at Carthage, Rome, and Milan before his conversion to Christianity, which he later narrated in the Confessions. He was ordained and eventually became bishop of Hippo Regius, where he wrote and preached until his death during the Vandal siege of the city. His thought developed across several controversies: against the Manichaeans, whom he had earlier followed, he defended the goodness of creation and treated evil as a privation of good rather than an independent substance; against the Donatists he argued for the catholicity and unity of the church and the validity of sacraments apart from the personal worthiness of the minister; and in the anti-Pelagian debates, against Pelagius and later Julian of Eclanum, he articulated the positions for which he became known in the Western tradition, namely the transmission of original sin, the bondage of the human will apart from grace, and the priority and gratuity of divine grace in salvation. In De Trinitate he developed an account of the triune God and explored analogies for the Trinity in the structure of the human mind. In De Civitate Dei, written after the sack of Rome in 410, he distinguished the earthly city from the city of God and set out a theology of history. His treatment of memory, time, desire, and the inner self in the Confessions and elsewhere has been read as foundational for later Western reflection on human interiority. The extent and interpretation of his doctrine of predestination and grace remained contested among his heirs, and later Western debates, including those of the Reformation, appealed to him on multiple sides.
editorial_notes: Birth and death years (354-430 CE) are as standardly reported and given in the lifespan note. Augustine is described here on Christian terms, as the tradition receives him, per neutrality principle #6. His doctrine of grace and predestination is reported as his attributed position, not adjudicated; the note records that his later readers, including in the Reformation, appealed to him on multiple sides, in keeping with the policy against flattening a live debate. A cross-tradition compares_with link to al-Ghazali is included per ADR-0005: it rests on a genuine and much-discussed scholarly parallel between the Confessions and al-Ghazali's al-Munqidh min al-Dalal as first-person accounts of intellectual crisis and turn toward God, and on the shared prominence of grace and the will; the note names the difference (Augustine's specifically anti-Pelagian doctrine of original sin and grace against al-Ghazali's Ash'ari framework), and asserts only the comparability of the question, not equivalence of answer. Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality principle #8: (a) a modern academic biography (Peter Brown, UC Press), (b) Augustine's own primary text (the Confessions), and (c) a contemporary theologian's treatment (Rowan Williams, On Augustine). Confidence is medium: sources verified to exist and be on-topic via publisher and library catalogues and peer-reviewed reviews, but relevant passages not read line-by-line.

lifespan.note: 354-430 CE; born at Thagaste, died at Hippo Regius in Roman North Africa. Years as standardly reported.

relations.note: Developed the doctrine of the transmission of original sin in the anti-Pelagian works; reported as his attributed position.
Argued for the priority and gratuity of divine grace and the bondage of the will apart from it.
Opposed Pelagius and Julian of Eclanum over grace, free will, and original sin; the critique is attributed to him, not adjudicated.
Wrote De Trinitate, developing an account of the triune God and mental analogies for it.

Both wrote influential first-person accounts of intellectual crisis and turn toward God (Augustine's Confessions and al-Ghazali's al-Munqidh min al-Dalal) and both gave grace and the will a central place; they differ in tradition and framework, Augustine within an anti-Pelagian Christian doctrine of original sin and grace, al-Ghazali within Ash'ari kalam. This asserts the comparability of the question, not the equivalence of the answer.

sources.citation: Brown, Peter. Augustine of Hippo: A Biography. Revised edition with a new epilogue. Berkeley: University of California Press, 2000.
